channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Analysis Suggests School Was Hit Amid U.S. Strikes on Iranian Naval Base
item.description: The Feb. 28 school strike in Minab, which killed dozens, including children, appears to have been part of an attack on an adjacent naval base in southern Iran, where officials said U.S. forces were operating.
item.dc:creator: Malachy Browne and Aaron Boxerman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Planet Labs, Rafaela Balster/The New York Times

item.title: As Trump Out-Putins Putin, Russia’s Global Influence Erodes
item.description: The conflict in Iran may give Moscow a short-term boost economically and in Ukraine. But it has also shown the limits of Russia’s partnerships.
item.dc:creator: Paul Sonne
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Maxim Shipenkov
item.media:description: President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia went to a wreath-laying ceremony at the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier in Moscow last month.

item.title: India Is Turning Back to Russian Oil
item.description: India’s trade deal with President Trump was supposed to end its imports of oil from Russia. But then the conflict in the Middle East has cut off alternative supplies.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Yoruk Isik/Reuters
item.media:description: A Russian tanker in 2024 navigating the Bosporus, one route used to export oil to buyers abroad.

item.title: Timeline: The Rise and Fall of Kristi Noem as Trump’s DHS Secretary
item.description: The homeland security secretary, who was fired by President Trump Thursday, helped fulfill his border pledges, but also drew negative attention to his administration.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs and Hamed Aleaziz
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Ms. Noem testified on Capitol Hill this week.

item.title: Trump Announces He Is Replacing Noem With Oklahoma Senator
item.description: The president said he would replace Kristi Noem, the homeland security secretary, with Senator Markwayne Mullin, after Ms. Noem was grilled by Republicans at a congressional hearing.
item.dc:creator: Michael C. Bender, Michael Gold, Hamed Aleaziz and Maggie Haberman
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kristi Noem testifying before lawmakers this week on Capitol Hill.

item.title: As Noem Speaks in Nashville, Senate Swarms Mullin
item.description: The remarkable split-screen underscored how abruptly Kristi Noem’s ouster as homeland security secretary had unfolded, even by the standards of President Trump’s fast-paced Washington.
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson

item.title: Senator Blumenthal Pushes to Investigate Kristi Noem for Perjury
item.description: Senator Richard Blumenthal of Connecticut said Democrats had evidence to suggest Kristi Noem lied under oath during a Senate hearing on Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse and Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Richard Blumenthal speaking on Capitol Hill in January. Blumenthal said that Kristi Noem’s removal did not protect her from an investigation.

item.title: DOJ Releases Missing Interviews With Woman Who Made Claims Against Trump in Epstein Files
item.description: The pages had been withheld from the trove of documents related to the convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein because of what officials called a mistaken determination that they were duplicates.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Michael A. McCoy for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Justice Department acknowledged on Thursday that it had also identified about a dozen other documents that were “incorrectly coded as duplicative.”

item.title: Lindsey Halligan Is Under Investigation by the Florida Bar
item.description: The actions of Ms. Halligan, who as a U.S. attorney brought criminal cases against President Trump’s enemies, are under review by the organization that licensed her to practice law.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett and Jonah E. Bromwich
item.media:credit: Jacquelyn Martin/Associated Press
item.media:description: Lindsey Halligan at the White House last August.

item.title: U.S. Restarts Diplomatic Relations With Venezuela
item.description: Washington has rapidly warmed up ties with Venezuela — and applied major pressure on it — since capturing the country’s president, Nicolás Maduro, two months ago.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: A protest last month in Caracas, Venezuela’s capital, demanding freedom for political prisoners.

item.title: Pentagon Officially Notifies Anthropic It Is a ‘Supply Chain Risk’
item.description: Anthropic has said it will sue the Defense Department over the designation, which could prevent the start-up from doing business with the U.S. government.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz, Julian E. Barnes and Sheera Frenkel
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Although Anthropic is the only company that provides the Pentagon with artificial intelligence technologies for use on classified systems, other companies are angling to replace it.

item.title: Representative Tony Gonzales Will Not Seek Re-election
item.description: Several House members had called for Mr. Gonzales, a Texas Republican, to step down after texts emerged showing his pursuit of a staff member who later killed herself.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Samuel Corum/Getty Images
item.media:description: Representative Tony Gonzales at a House Homeland Security Committee hearing in Washington last month.

item.title: Steve Daines’s Switcheroo Starts New Fight in Montana Senate Race
item.description: Minutes before the Senate filing deadline, Senator Steve Daines withdrew his re-election bid and an ally jumped in. Even some fellow Republicans criticized the 11th-hour switch.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Thom Bridge/Independent Record, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Kurt Alme, who has been serving as the U.S. attorney for Montana, was endorsed by Senator Steve Daines and President Trump to succeed Mr. Daines in a race for what will now be an open seat.

item.title: Lev Parnas Running for Congress as a Democrat in Florida
item.description: Mr. Parnas, who once tried to help find damaging information on Hunter Biden, said his background had turned him into “an advocate for speaking truth to power.”
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei
item.category: Trump-Ukraine Whistle-Blower Complaint and Impeachment Inquiry
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lev Parnas announced his campaign in a video and post on Substack on Wednesday.

item.title: An Assertive Supreme Court Turns to Curbing State Courts
item.description: Liberal justices accused their colleagues of expanding use of the emergency docket again in two orders issued this week.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Since President Trump returned to the White House, the Supreme Court’s conservative majority has shown a willingness to short-circuit lower-court proceedings.

item.title: A Judge’s ‘Battle Royale’ With Trump and the Supreme Court
item.description: After repeated but cryptic rebukes from the justices, Judge Brian Murphy last week again ruled against one of the administration’s signature immigration programs.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Getty Images/Getty Images
item.media:description: Camp Lemonnier in Djibouti.

item.title: The Good Times Are Over in China’s Liquor Capital
item.description: Once celebrated for its fiery spirits, the town of Maotai has reeled from a bad Chinese economy, changing tastes and a crackdown on boozy official banquets.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Higgins and Gilles Sabrié
item.media:description: Tanks of fermented sorghum at Jinjiao Distillery in Maotai, China, in February.

item.title: He Had a Purple Heart, PTSD and a Rap Sheet. He Had to Leave the U.S.
item.description: After a long battle with drug addiction, Sae Joon Park felt settled in a new life. But he was deported last year and is now fighting to get back to Hawaii.
item.dc:creator: Max Kim
item.media:credit: Jean Chung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sae Joon Park in his apartment in Busan, South Korea, in February. Despite living in the U.S. for nearly 50 years, Park was ordered to move back to South Korea in June 2025.

item.title: Jamil Jivani, a Friend of JD Vance, Channels Charlie Kirk With Young Men
item.description: Jamil Jivani, a Conservative rookie member of Parliament, follows an American playbook to win over young men on college campuses.
item.dc:creator: Norimitsu Onishi
item.media:credit: Gavin John for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jamil Jivani, a conservative member of Parliament, talking with supporters after speaking at the University of Calgary last month.

item.title: Senator Tim Sheehy Helps Officers Forcibly Remove Protesting Veteran From Hearing
item.description: The physical struggle, involving Senator Tim Sheehy of Montana, was captured on video. The protester, Brian McGinnis, said on social media that his arm was broken.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Andrew Harnik/Getty Images
item.media:description: Brian McGinnis, a Marine veteran from North Carolina, was protesting military actions in Iran when he was forcibly removed from a Senate hearing by Capitol Police officers and Senator Tim Sheehy on Wednesday.

item.title: A Killing Spree in Utah Rattles a Region and Puzzles Officials
item.description: Three women were slain in two locations, officials said on Thursday. A frantic manhunt involving four states led to an arrest, but a motive was still unclear.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy and Zak Podmore
item.media:credit: George Frey/Getty Images
item.media:description: Torrey, Utah, in 2023. The authorities asked residents there and in surrounding areas to lock their doors and stay home on Thursday before they arrested a suspect in the killing of three women.

item.title: How $800 Monthly Car Payments Are Hurting Car Sales
item.description: Cars have become so expensive that many Americans are putting off or not buying new cars, hurting the auto industry.
item.dc:creator: Neal E. Boudette
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Sports Utility Vehicles and Light Trucks
item.media:credit: Jeff Roberson/Associated Press
item.media:description: The average monthly payment for a new car was $774 at the end of last year, and the average new car now costs nearly $50,000.

item.title: Fewer Pregnant Women Received Acetaminophen in E.R.s After White House Warnings
item.description: A new analysis of prescription data found that emergency room orders for acetaminophen for pregnant women fell for weeks after the federal warning.
item.dc:creator: Azeen Ghorayshi
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump made an announcement about autism and Tylenol alongside Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. in the Roosevelt Room of the White House in Washington last September.

item.title: The Boys in Trueblood Came to Rock
item.description: The four teenage brothers mostly play the oldies. (To them, the oldies are Coldplay and Maroon 5.)
item.dc:creator: Callie Holtermann and Elinor Kry

item.title: America Won’t Save Iran
item.description: The most likely outcome of the American-Israeli bombing campaign is not the advent of liberal democracy but something worse.
item.dc:creator: Reza Aslan
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Photo illustration by The New York Times; source photograph by Atta Kenare/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images

item.title: What Kristi Noem Should Do After President Trump Fired Her
item.description: It’s hard out there for a MAGA woman.
item.dc:creator: Michelle Cottle
item.media:credit: Damon Winter/The New York Times

item.title: Late Night Isn’t Sad to See Kristi Noem Leave Homeland Security
item.description: “Sounds like someone’s about to become the FIFA secretary of homeland security,” Stephen Colbert joked.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Not Just Oil: In Venezuela, U.S. Interior Secretary Pushes for Mining Access
item.description: Venezuela is home to large reserves of rare earths, gold and other valuable minerals that the Trump administration wants to exert more control over.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak and Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Federico Parra/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Interior Secretary Doug Burgum and Delcy Rodríguez, Venezuela’s new leader, in Caracas on Wednesday.

item.title: Racist, Antisemitic Slurs Surface in Florida GOP Group Chat
item.description: After the secretary of the Miami-Dade County Republican Party created the chat for college students, it devolved into slurs against Black and Jewish people.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Martina Tuaty for The New York Times
item.media:description: The WhatsApp chat was created last fall for students at Florida International University in Miami.

item.title: Ronnie Eldridge, NY Politician and Aide to RFK and Lindsay, Dies at 95
item.description: She was an adviser to Senator Robert F. Kennedy, Mayor John V. Lindsay and Representative Bella S. Abzug before serving on the New York City Council from 1989 to 2001.
item.dc:creator: Sam Roberts
item.media:credit: Marilynn K. Yee/The New York Times
item.media:description: Ronnie Eldridge at a City Council meeting in 1991. Two decades earlier, while working as a top aide to Mayor John V. Lindsay, she was described by The Times as “one of the most influential women of politics in the city government.”

item.title: Superfood Fuels Mating Frenzy for Critically Endangered Kakapo
item.description: Kakapos, which are reclusive and flightless and can live as long as humans, are found only in New Zealand. They feed on the fruit of the rimu tree.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.media:credit: Andrew Digby/Dept. of Conservation, New Zealand, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A kakapo with eggs on Anchor Island, in New Zealand. The island is known for lacking the kinds of predators that threaten the parrots.
